more conservative workers, and
Mrs. Wells organized a club of fol-
lowerds, composed of the more in
dependent thinkers known as the
Progressive Woman's Suffrage
union.


With Mrs. Wells as leader this
body of women inaugurated the
street corner meetings. The move
ment gradually grew in popularity,
and other members joined the rank
and file. Five meetings in all have
been held and each has attracted
hundreds of spectators, mostly Gu
riosity seekers.


The campaign is preparatory to
an appeal. granting woman full suf-
frage, to be made at Albany Feb.
10, when the suffragets will attend
in a body.


In discussing the open air meet
ings, Mrs. Wells said:


THE meetings have been par
ticularly orderly and the specta-
tors have been most respectful. 1t
takes sensational demonstration to
arouse the women to action. | do
not want to be put in the attitude
of criticising the American women,
but they are too mild in their
